input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Infrastructure assets, such as buildings, within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by complex and heterogeneous environments requiring integrated virtual representations for lifecycle management from initial design to operational phases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, simulation, analysis, and optimization for infrastructure management. Target users are stakeholders across the AECO domain, enabling collaboration and data-driven decision-making throughout the building's lifecycle from design to refurbishments and operations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points for service integration, centralized hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange, and coordination among Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers roles for holistic building lifecycle management
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs BIM-derived geometric models augmented with semantic data for Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers), establishing a unified semantic foundation. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are utilized to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models, enabling automated generation of deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data exchange synchronizes physical building states with the digital twin, supported by interactive visualization for monitoring and control operations.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Evaluation of model fidelity is performed using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to measure how well the resulting Digital Twin reflects its physical counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange using Eclipse Hono and Ditto for continuous synchronization between physical assets and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals transmitted to Building Control Systems via Ditto as part of bidirectional data exchange.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Technical infrastructure utilizing Eclipse Hono and Ditto for interoperable real-time data exchange between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment leveraging Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM environments, automated deployment through Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to generate deployable artifacts, real-time operation with continuous data synchronization and interactive visualization for monitoring and control, and evolution via modular integration of custom services and analytics to adapt to changing operational needs. Stakeholders such as Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers each play specific roles across these phases.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. A modular architecture with standardized extension points supports the integration of custom services and analytics, enabling the Digital Twin to evolve in response to operational changes. Quality assurance, testing, validation, and version control practices are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides real-time data exchange for continuous synchronization between physical and digital assets, interactive visualization for immersive monitoring and control interfaces, and analytics capabilities through custom services. However, specific insight types, decision-making processes, and confidence measures for recommendations are not explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework ensures compatibility with existing AECO-specific systems and standards, including BIM, which is recognized as the standard solution in the construction industry, addressing BIM's limitations as a seamless enabler for dynamic Digital Twin deployments.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted description accurately reflects the general type of system discussed (buildings and infrastructure in the AECO domain) and the complexity of their environments. It correctly captures the lifecycle management aspect. However, it focuses on the general domain rather than the specific case study system (the Living Lab) mentioned in the text, and misses specific operational details found in the research context (e.g., lighting systems). Supported by quotes: 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain', 'complex and heterogeneous infrastructure environments', 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extraction is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the text. It correctly identifies a wide range of services including monitoring, simulation, optimization, and predictive analytics, as well as the target users (stakeholders) and the lifecycle scope. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics', 'stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life cycle'.

The extraction precisely names the specific software frameworks and engines (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) and their correct functions as stated in the abstract and introduction. Supported by the quote: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.'

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the system's temporal capabilities as 'real-time' and 'continuous synchronization' based on the provided text. It captures the essence of the data exchange described, although specific metrics like latency or frequency are not detailed in the source.

The document describes a framework for deploying Digital Twins and the architecture of a specific DT instance, but does not contain information regarding multiplicities, multiple instances, or hierarchical structures between twins.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the system's orchestration, including the modular architecture, the role of the framework as a central hub, and the coordination of specific stakeholder roles. It is strongly grounded in the source text.

The source text discusses the integration of internal components (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) and BIM models, but does not provide details on integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards with other systems, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and perfectly supported by the source text. It accurately describes the use of BIM as a semantic foundation, the application of MDE principles for artifact generation, and the real-time data exchange with visualization. Supported by quotes: 'Our approach automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts', and 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital counterparts'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions modeling actuators within BIM ('define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment') and references 'lighting systems', it does not provide the specific physical details required by the characteristic description (types, control capabilities, ranges, safety constraints). The information present pertains to the modeling framework rather than the physical component specifications.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text mentions 'sensors' in the context of modeling ('define sensors, actuators, and controllers') and data usage ('post-occupancy sensor data'), but does not describe the physical sensing infrastructure as requested (sensor types, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, accuracy). Therefore, the specific details for this characteristic are absent.

The extraction precisely identifies the specific method used for evaluation in the text, naming the metric and its purpose. Supported by quotes: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents mention 'governance' in the context of organizational policies and architectural decisions (e.g., 'MDE also strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies...'), but they do not contain specific information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or data anonymization. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The text describes the system architecture and components like Eclipse Hono and Ditto for 'real-time data exchange' and mentions extracting metadata such as 'tenant and device ID'. However, it does not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation, or fail-safe mechanisms. The extraction 'Not in Document' accurately reflects the absence of these specific safety and security details in the provided text.
